flashcards.Back: A decentralized management model where each network device (router/switch) contains its own control plane and is configured individually.
A centralized management model where a software controller manages multiple network devices, often centralizing the control plane functions.
The model used in traditional networking where each device independently runs routing protocols (like OSPF) to make its own forwarding decisions.
The model used in SDN where the intelligence and decision-making for the whole network are moved to a single software controller.
An architectural approach that makes networks agile and flexible by separating the control plane from the underlying data plane.
The API that allows a network controller to communicate with higher-level applications and scripts for automation and orchestration.
The API or protocol (like OpenFlow or NETCONF) that a controller uses to communicate with and configure physical network devices.
The part of a network device's architecture that is responsible for moving actual user packets from port to port based on the control plane's instructions.
The logical area of a network device that handles the 'intelligence'—learning MAC addresses, building routing tables, and running STP.
The logical area of a device used for administrative tasks, such as SSH, Telnet, or SNMP access.
One of the first and most well-known southbound protocols, used to allow a controller to directly manipulate the forwarding tables of a switch.
An IETF standard southbound protocol that uses XML-based data encoding to install, manipulate, and delete configurations on network devices.
An HTTP-based protocol that provides a RESTful interface for managing network devices, often used as a modern alternative to NETCONF.
Cisco's enterprise SDN controller that provides a centralized platform for managing campus networks using software-defined principles.
A networking model where the administrator defines what they want the network to do (intent), and the controller automatically handles the 'how'.
Cisco's software-defined solution for campus LANs, automating the configuration of VLANs, security, and segmentation through DNA Center.
A software-defined approach to managing wide-area networks, providing centralized control over multiple connection types like MPLS and internet.
The automated coordination and management of complex network tasks and workflows across multiple systems and devices.
Cisco's SDN solution for data centers, which uses a spine-leaf topology and a centralized APIC controller.
Representational State Transfer. the most common type of Northbound API, allowing software to interact with network controllers using standard web methods.
A benefit of SDN where the controller hides the complex individual CLI commands of different devices and presents a unified management interface.
The process where a controller pushes a single security or QoS policy out to all relevant devices in the network simultaneously.
The hardware table in a switch (FIB) that is updated by the centralized controller in an SDN architecture.
A popular open-source SDN controller framework that supports a wide range of southbound and northbound protocols.
A risk in traditional networks where proprietary features and management tools make it difficult to switch to a different hardware manufacturer.
A Cisco-developed southbound protocol used in ACI to communicate policy information between the APIC controller and the switches.
The enhanced ability of an SDN controller to see the state and traffic patterns of the entire network from a single dashboard.
In traditional networks, the CPU resources consumed by each device to run its own routing and management protocols.
A specific URL on a network controller that a script or application connects to in order to send or receive management data.
A network that uses both traditional distributed control protocols and a centralized SDN controller simultaneously.
